description: After partner opens 1-of-a-suit and the opponent overcalls, responder's double is conventional — showing values and unbid suits rather than penalty. Opener rebids after `after-neg-dbl` are synthesized at load time in `crates/bridge-conventions/src/registry/module_registry.rs`.
purpose: Keep the bidding alive when opponents overcall, showing the unbid suits (especially majors) that a natural response would have shown

authority.label: Larry Cohen — authoritative on modern negative double practice and level-dependent thresholds

teaching.tradeoff: Using double as conventional means you can't make a penalty double of the overcall at low levels.
teaching.principle: When opponents overcall, double shows the values and suits (especially unbid majors) that a natural response would show, keeping the bidding alive at a low level.

commonMistakes.text: Do not treat a negative double as a penalty double.
commonMistakes.reason: The call shows values and unbid suits; pure penalty intentions start with pass and later conversion, not an immediate negative double.

commonMistakes.text: Do not negative-double with a 5+ card suit you could bid naturally.
commonMistakes.reason: A natural suit bid usually describes your shape more accurately than hiding that suit inside a conventional double.

commonMistakes.text: Do not negative-double with a penalty-quality holding in the overcalled suit.
commonMistakes.reason: When your strength sits in their suit, the partnership wants a penalty route instead of a takeout-style description.

commonMistakes.text: Do not assume every negative double shows both majors.
commonMistakes.reason: Promises vary by auction; for example, after 1♥-(1♠) the negative double typically shows the minors rather than an unbid major.

9+ HCP for negative double at 2-level

4+ cards in at least one major

scope.reason: Opener rebids after a negative double are synthesized into route-specific opener states at load time in crates/bridge-conventions/src/registry/module_registry.rs.

summaryCard.trigger: Partner opens one of a suit, right-hand opponent overcalls, and you double conventionally
summaryCard.partnership: This module follows Cohen's all-level negative-double treatment. The exact suit promise still changes with the opening, overcall, and level.

whenToUse.gloss: At the one-level, use the negative double with competitive values and an unbid four-card major that a natural response would have shown.
At the two-level, negative doubles need more values and may show either an unbid major or both minors, depending on the exact overcall.
Over especially space-eating overcalls such as two spades after a minor opening, treat the negative double as a stronger action rather than an automatic reflex.

workedAuctions.label: One-level overcall: double keeps the unbid major in play

calls.rationale: Opener starts with a natural one-diamond opening.
Negative double shows spades and competitive values more economically than inventing another call.
Opener can now rebid naturally with a balanced minimum and no spade fit.

workedAuctions.label: Counter-example: a real five-card suit is often better bid naturally

calls.rationale: With a real five-card spade suit, bidding it naturally is often more descriptive than hiding it inside a negative double.

items.opponentAction: The opener's side raises the overcall after your negative double
items.ourAction: Let opener reevaluate naturally between supporting the implied suit, rebidding notrump, competing further, or passing with a minimum and no fit.
items.note: The negative double described your hand shape; it did not force opener to act again at all costs.

items.opponentAction: The auction gets doubled or redoubled later
items.ourAction: Treat later doubles as natural or partnership-specific; the original double has already spent the conventional meaning.
items.note: Once the negative double is on the table, later doubles need a separate agreement.

items.recommendation: With weak hands, prefer a natural suit bid or pass; the negative double is not the default catch-all.
items.note: If you can bid a real five-card suit economically, do that instead of forcing a generic sequence through the double.

items.recommendation: Core auctions to memorize: one club over one diamond double shows both majors; one diamond over one heart double shows at least four spades; one heart over one spade double shows both minors.
items.note: Those anchor sequences show why there is no universal negative-double promise; the exact meaning depends on the auction.

items.recommendation: With stronger hands, the same sequence map still applies, but the negative double often starts a forcing auction instead of ending the description.
items.note: Use the response table for the exact continuation because the suit promise remains sequence-specific even when the hand is stronger.

relatedLinks.discriminator: When you hold a real five-card suit that can be bid economically, the natural call may describe the hand better than a negative double.

teaching.purpose: When opponents overcall partner's opening bid, use a conventional double to show the unbid suits (especially majors) and competitive values instead of passing or overbidding
teaching.whenToUse: Partner opens 1C/1D/1H/1S and RHO overcalls. You have 6+ HCP (at 1-level), about 9+ HCP on the 2-level, and stronger competitive values as the overcall climbs, plus length in the unbid suits.
whenNotToUse: You have a natural bid available (5+ card suit you can bid at the appropriate level)
You want to penalize the overcaller — pass and hope partner reopens with a double
The exact auction does not fit your hand — negative doubles stay sequence-specific even when the overcall comes high
teaching.tradeoff: Negative doubles give up the penalty double of the overcall at low levels in exchange for showing the unbid suits efficiently
teaching.principle: After an overcall, double shows the unbid suits (especially unbid majors) with competitive values. The specific suits shown depend on which suits are already bid.
teaching.roles: Responder describes shape via double; opener evaluates fit and rebids accordingly

definitions.description: Minimum HCP for negative double at 1-level
Minimum HCP for negative double at 2-level
Has 4+ cards in at least one major
